## Email 1 — Teaser (T-14 days | Send: 2026-05-01)

**Subject line:** A question about your codebase
*(28 characters ✓)*

**Preview text:** Most engineers answer it the same way. There's a better way.
*(61 characters ✓)*

**Opening line:**
There's a question every engineer gets asked — or asks themselves — at least once a week.

**Body:**

"Where does X happen in this codebase?"

Where does the authentication logic actually live? Where is that error handled? Who calls this function and from how many places?

If you're lucky, someone on the team knows. You find them, you interrupt their work, they walk you through it. If you're not lucky, you spend an hour tracing it yourself — grepping for function names, following import chains, hoping you didn't miss a branch.

That question is slow to answer every single time. And the worst part: you already answered it last month for a different part of the same codebase, and you'll answer it again next month.

We've been building something about this problem. Something that makes "where does X happen" take seconds, not an hour — with an answer you can verify, not just trust.

Stay tuned — more soon.

**CTA:** Hit reply if this sounds familiar

**P.S.** — We've been testing with engineering teams for the past two months. The reaction has been consistent: "I didn't know this was possible." More in a few days.

## Email 2 — Problem Agitation (T-10 days | Send: 2026-05-05)

**Subject line:** The "where does X happen" problem
*(35 characters ✓)*

**Preview text:** It's more expensive than you think — and it happens more often.
*(63 characters ✓)*

**Opening line:**
Here's something that came up in every engineering team we interviewed last year.

**Body:**

We asked 40 engineers a simple question: "In the last sprint, how many hours did you spend understanding code you didn't write, before you could change it?"

The average answer: 6 hours. Per sprint. Per engineer.

That's not onboarding time — they were past onboarding. That's just the cost of working in a codebase that doesn't explain itself. Every code review, every incident, every "add one feature to a module you've never touched" — all of them involve the same slow manual process of understanding before acting.

One senior engineer told us: "I've been on this team for two years. I still discover parts of the codebase I didn't know existed every week. And I still have to trace everything manually because there's no other way."

We've been building the other way.

It indexes your repo. It knows what's in it. And it answers questions with evidence — pointing back to the exact lines in your code. Not guesses. Not hallucinated paths. Real answers, from your real codebase.

A blurred preview: [screenshot descriptor — blurred chat interface showing a question and a cited answer, file name and line number visible but text redacted]

Full reveal in 3 days.

**CTA:** Join the early access list

## Email 3 — The Reveal (T-7 days | Send: 2026-05-08)

**Subject line:** Here's what we've been building
*(31 characters ✓)*

**Preview text:** RepoBrain — ask questions about any codebase, get cited answers.
*(65 characters ✓)*

**Opening line:**
The problem we described last week? We built the fix.

**Body:**

Introducing **RepoBrain**.

RepoBrain indexes your Git repository and lets you ask questions about it in natural language. "Where does authentication happen?" "What calls this function?" "Find error handlers without retry logic." Every answer cites the exact file:line it came from — so you can verify any claim in one click.

The mechanism that makes this work: three retrieval strategies run in parallel on every question. Semantic search finds conceptually similar code. Lexical search matches keywords. Structural search follows symbol relations — calls, imports, uses. The results merge and rank. The LLM's context is built from what's actually in your repo — not from training data, not from guesses.

That's why the answers have citations. The AI only uses what retrieval returned. It can't hallucinate a path that doesn't exist.

Beyond conversations, RepoBrain ships with 15 features: blast radius analysis, natural language search, pattern detection, architecture diagrams, Git timeline, specialist agents for code review and security audits, and more — all working from the same indexed repo.

**Watch the 90-second walkthrough:** [video link or "see the demo at repobrain.dev"]

Early access is open now. The first 100 teams get founding price.

**CTA:** Get early access →

**P.S.** — Early access slots are limited to 100 teams before we open publicly. You're near the front of the list. Don't sit on it.

## Email 4 — Proof + Scarcity (T-3 days | Send: 2026-05-12)

**Subject line:** What beta teams said (the honest ones)
*(38 characters ✓)*

**Preview text:** Not "great tool!" — one specific thing that surprised them most.
*(66 characters ✓)*

**Opening line:**
We asked our beta teams one question: "What surprised you most?"

**Body:**

We didn't ask "would you recommend this?" or "what's your overall rating?" We asked what surprised them. Here's what came back:

[TESTIMONIAL NEEDED — specific outcome + named person + company. Target format: "We used RepoBrain to [specific action] and [specific result]. — [Name], [Title], [Company]"]

The part that consistently surprises engineers: the citations are real. They expect an AI to give a plausible-sounding answer. They don't expect to click the citation and land on the exact line — and have it be right.

One engineer told us they'd spent 45 minutes the previous day tracing an auth flow manually. They asked RepoBrain the same question the next morning. Answer in 12 seconds. Three citations, all accurate.

That's not a promise. That's what the beta data looks like.

**We have 37 early access slots remaining** at founding price ($29/mo Pro, locked for life). After May 15th, Pro is $49/mo.

## Email 5 — Launch Day (T-0 | Send: 2026-05-15, morning)

**Subject line:** RepoBrain is live
*(17 characters ✓)*

**Preview text:** Index your first repo free — founding price ends in 48 hours.
*(62 characters ✓)*

**Opening line:**
Today is the day.

**Body:**

RepoBrain is officially open.

Connect any GitHub repo. Index it for free. Ask your first question — and see for yourself what a sourced answer to a code question looks like.

If you've been waiting: this is the moment. Go to **repobrain.dev**, authorize GitHub, and you can have your first repo indexed in under 10 minutes.

What you get on the free tier: Code Conversations, Natural Language Search, Blast Radius, and Architecture Diagrams — for one repo, with no time limit.

What Pro unlocks: all 15 features, up to 10 repos, 3 team members, and specialist agents for code review, security audits, and ADR generation.

**Founding price:** Pro is $29/mo for the first 100 teams who sign up today or tomorrow. After midnight on Friday, May 17th, Pro goes to $49/mo permanently. This isn't artificial scarcity — we're locking in a cohort of early teams at a lower rate as a thank-you for betting on us early.

*A note from the founder:*

I built RepoBrain because "where does X happen" used to take me a day. I wanted it to take 12 seconds — with an answer I could verify. It does that now. I hope it does the same for you.

— Robin

**CTA:** Index your first repo free →

**P.S.** — We're also launching on Product Hunt today. If you find RepoBrain useful, an upvote goes a long way: [Product Hunt link]. Every vote in the first 3 hours matters.
